text:
This paper offers a comparative view of the way different Learning Management Systems are implemented in higher education and its evolution over time. While the digital infrastructure that is entrenched in the USA is compared to the emerging dynamics of e-learning on the African continent, reflections are sought that can enable us to understand the pedagogical mainstreaming of the plethora of learning resources such as Learning Management Systems (LMS) and MOOCs at the heart of the USA pedagogic practice, against the backdrop of immense structural, economic and cultural challenges that African institutions have to contend with. This study also identifies the challenges that each of the continents have to contend with; ranging from digital exclusion, access to reliable internet, cost affordability of data for accessing learning materials and more prospectively the manner in which public-private partnerships and regional integration are emerging as means of improving access to electronic learning systems across the two continents.

By analyzing policy and funding frameworks across European countries and several African nations, this review sheds light on the factors affecting the speed of adoption of technology-enhanced education practices, and the extent to which technology-supported learning opportunities can address the social equity concerns associated with digital education in Africa. The review also reflects on the upsurge of the use of individualized learning approaches and the blended learning approach and their associated impacts on learners’ engagement. In a context of rising globalization, this paper provides a global review of the on-going changes in technology enhanced learning happening at different scales, and hence offers a valuable knowledge resource for governments, education leaders and managers aiming to develop a modern and inclusive digital learning environment that connects learners locally, nationally and globally.
